title: Identifying Validation For each A and B pair, check the more effective response. • •

pages.text:
157
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Interpersonal Effectiveness Handout 18a
Identifying Validation
For each A and B pair, check the more effective response.
•
•
•
•
•
•


 1A.	Think about your day when the other
person is talking about his or her day.

 1B.	Throw yourself into listening about the
other person’s day.
••••••••

 5A.	Remember that people’s thoughts,
feelings, and behaviors don’t always
match. Check the facts.

 5B.	Assume that you can tell exactly what
people are feeling and thinking.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•


 2A.	If you are uncertain of people’s
thoughts and feelings, ask them what
they are thinking or feeling, or try to
imagine yourself in their situation.

 2B.	Assume that if people want you
to know what they are thinking or
feeling, they will tell you.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•


 6A.	Evaluate other people’s behaviors and
thoughts, and tell them that they are
wrong or that they should be different
when you feel sure you are right.

 6B.	If you do not agree with what another
person is doing or thinking, try to
understand how it could make sense if
you understood the causes.
•
•
•
•
•
•


 3A.	Observe the small clues that indicate
what is going on in social situations.

 3B.	Observe only what people say, and
ignore nonverbal signals.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•


 7A.	Assume that if you tell a person his
or her request of you makes sense,
that’s all you have to do to validate the
person.

 7B.	When a person asks you for
something, giving the person what
has been asked for is validation.
•
•
•
•
•
•


 4A.	Jump to conclusions about what
people mean.

 4B.	Realize that the same behavior can
mean many things.
••••••••

 8A.	Assume that other people’s reactions
to you have nothing to do with yours to
them.

 8B.	Treat each person with respect and as
an equal.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Identifying Validation For each A and B pair, check the more effective response. • •
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Use this to protect both people: needs are real, boundaries are kind, and a pause is not abandonment when repair is named.
tiny_steps: Name the ask in one sentence.
Agree on a return time or next check-in.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use text/chat if speech is hard.
Include caregiver capacity as part of the plan, not an afterthought.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use skills to win, corner, threaten leaving, or make either person prove pain or disability.
